Alerts & notifications
Critical — act now
DATP Public Comment reading room is live. The City of Colorado Springs Digital Accessibility Transition Plan (Draft 2026), with the full plan embedded as a PDF and twelve structured public comments residents can adapt and submit. Comment portal links live in the reading room.
Public comment · COS Digital Accessibility Transition Plan · May 2026
Open the DATP reading room →
BOCC petition — service plan transparency & algorithmic pricing disclosure. A general resident petition with four narrow asks within existing BOCC authority. Sign and send.
Civic action · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
Open petition letter →
Veterans' petition to the EPC BOCC. Companion petition for veterans of any branch — service plan transparency and algorithmic pricing protections, framed around military housing harms.
Civic action · Veterans · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
Open veterans' petition →
LDC update — public comment on service plan transparency. A 300-word comment ready to submit through the El Paso County Land Development Code update process.
Public comment · LDC update · El Paso County · May 2026
Open LDC comment →
Background brief — algorithmic rent pricing in El Paso County. Sourced reference for journalists, policy analysts, and the COS Chamber housing committee: federal antitrust action, local policy options, and the case for BOCC-level service plan transparency.
Reference · Housing & pricing · El Paso County BOCC · May 2026
Read background brief →
HOME-ARP comment window is live — closes May 26, 2026. COS is collecting public comment on the HOME-ARP Allocation Plan amendment via an ArcGIS Survey123 form Esri's own ACR rates as "Partially Supports" on WCAG 2.1 A/AA. A WCAG 2.1 AA Drupal Webform replacement is ready to install — bilingual EN/ES, JSON webhook + staff email + native storage, inline accommodation intake.
24 CFR 91.105 · ADA Title II · May 4, 2026
Open the accessible HTML form preview → Open the HOME-ARP bundle README →
Downtown Funding and Records Search Search BID assessments, DDA grants, TIF reimbursements, board meetings, and financial records in one place.
Annual adopted budget — C.R.S. 29-1-101 et seq · April 10, 2026
Open the search portal →
ADA Title II deadline: April 24, 2026 — 16 days. All local government websites must meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Zero district sites are confirmed compliant on PDF documents.
HB21-1110 · 28 C.F.R. Part 35 · April 8, 2026
Open district audit dashboard →
249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.
EPC Assessor audit · April 2026
See district audit →
WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.
Platform audit · April 2026
Draft compliance letter →
High priority
All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.
ADA document audit · April 2026
View ADA analysis →
0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.
Governance gap · §32-1-202
See audit dashboard →
Annual audit documents: publicly posted by 0 of 8 districts. Required by §32-1-106. Due to State Auditor by July 31. The clock is running.
Governance gap · §32-1-106 · due July 31
See audit dashboard →
Informational
2 CORA requests pending response. Military housing numbers and district governance records both awaiting reply. Response window is still open.
CORA tracker · April 2026
View tracker →
Wolfersberger compliance and Bellamese analysis complete. New findings ready for review on developer agreements, compliance history, and parcel-level records.
Compliance analysis · April 2026
Read analysis →
Jurisdiction finder is live. Enter any El Paso County address to find your metro districts, school district, city jurisdiction, and utilities — plain language, no login.
New · April 2026
Find my jurisdiction →
CSPD Blotter — Weekly History is live. 271 incidents pulled directly from the live blotter feed, paired with a Poisson 95% confidence band to flag statistical anomalies week to week. Tabs are full APG-pattern keyboard accessible; every chart has an aria-label.
New · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open blotter dashboard →
Lorson Ranch financial dashboard is live. Seven cross-pledged metro districts, $94.2M aggregate audited deficit, IGA money flows, and a per-home tax calculator — sourced from 2026 budgets and 2024 audits. Companion plain-language briefing available as a download.
New · Special Districts · April 2026
Open Lorson Ranch dashboard →
CBI Missing Persons form — accessible rebuild is live. Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. Drops into the existing Drupal Webform infrastructure with optional Claude API routing.
New · State · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open CBI form rebuild →
LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.
New · Private Sector · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open ADAM dashboard →

El Paso County population components of change

Real administrative record Census estimate — not a real count Derived / mixed
People added 2024–2025 est.
4,700
down from 5,975 prior year
Avg net domestic migration loss 2010s est.
-5,300/yr
people leaving for other US counties
Avg natural growth 2010s real
+7,400/yr
births minus deaths
Birth peak (early 1990s) real
16,000
now at 1980s levels despite +350k residents
Population change = natural growth + net migration — El Paso County estimates
Sources: Colorado State Demography Office (DOLA) — demography.dola.colorado.gov/births-deaths-migration. Natural growth (births − deaths) uses real vital record counts. Net migration is a Census Bureau estimate — not a count. These two are combined to produce total population change.
Decade averages — components of change
1990s avg natural growth per year real~12,000
1990s avg net domestic migration per year est.-7,500
1990s avg international migration per year est.+7,200
2010s avg natural growth per year real~7,400
2010s avg net domestic migration per year est.-5,300
2010s avg international migration per year est.+1,800
2021–2022 natural growth real3,448 (COVID deaths)
2024–2025 total population added est.4,700
El Paso County has had net domestic out-migration every decade going back to the 1990s. The county's population grew only because births and international immigration outpaced the people leaving. That structural pattern is now weakening — births are declining and international migration dropped 55% nationally in 2024–2025.
Births and deaths — real administrative records
Source: Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) Vital Statistics Program. Birth and death certificates are legally required filings — this is a real count, not a survey or estimate. Available by county at cdphe.colorado.gov. DOLA compiles these into annual county-level components of change.
Key birth and death facts
Births peaked (early 1990s)~16,000/yr
Current birth level (approx.)~8,000–9,000/yr
County population at birth peak~400,000
County population now~743,000
COVID death spike effect on natural growth~2x reduction 2021–22
65+ population growth 2021–2022 statewide+29,000
Births are real counted records — but they don't directly create new households. A birth adds to the population of an existing household. What creates housing demand is net new household formation — which requires both a person and a separate address. That figure is not tracked in any real-time administrative system.
Migration — the number nobody actually counts
There is no government system that counts who moves into or out of El Paso County each year. Net migration is a Census Bureau calculation — total population change minus natural growth — not a direct measurement. Nobody counts U-Hauls, change of address forms are not compiled by county, and driver's license transfers are not published.
Proxy sources that approximate migration — none are complete
USPS change of address forms by countyavailable — incomplete
IRS tax return address changes year over yearavailable — 1yr lag
Colorado DMV driver's license state transfersnot published by county
School enrollment change by district boundarypartial — children only
Voter registration new registrants by countypartial — registered voters only
CSU utility new accounts opened per yearCORA pending — best proxy
CSU utility accounts closed per yearCORA pending — best out-migration proxy
The CSU utility account data — new accounts opened and closed per year — is the closest thing to a real count of people moving in and out that exists at the county level. It captures actual physical residency changes, not survey estimates.
The Census Bureau's migration estimate is calculated as a residual — total population change minus births plus deaths. It absorbs all measurement error from every other estimate. It is not measured independently.
The military distortion — unique to El Paso County
Fort Carson, Peterson Space Force Base, Schriever Space Force Base, and the Air Force Academy collectively represent one of the largest military concentrations in the US. Military personnel and their families cycle through El Paso County on 2–3 year rotation orders — creating a population churn that looks identical to civilian in/out migration in Census estimates but is structurally different.
Military personnel on-post at Fort Carson live in federal housing not tracked by the county assessor, pay no property tax, and generate no utility account with CSU. They are present in the population but invisible to nearly every administrative data system used for planning.
When military relocations slow — as happened in 2024 per market reports — apartment absorption spikes, school enrollment shifts, and retail demand changes. But no planning system predicted this because military rotation data is federal and not shared with the county.
Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) paid to military families distorts rental market pricing — landlords price to BAH rates, pushing rents above what civilian incomes can support. When rotation orders change, that demand disappears without warning.
A FOIA request to the Department of the Army for Fort Carson annual on-post housing occupancy counts, and a similar request to Peterson/Schriever for off-post housing allowance recipients by ZIP code, would be the only way to account for this population in any real planning model.
Real data sources — births, deaths, migration
Annual births by county — real countCDPHE Vital Statistics — cdphe.colorado.gov
Annual deaths by county — real countCDPHE Vital Statistics — cdphe.colorado.gov
Births, deaths and migration lookup toolDOLA — demography.dola.colorado.gov/births-deaths-migration
IRS migration data (tax return address changes)IRS SOI — irs.gov/statistics/soi-tax-stats-migration-data
USPS change of address (national change of address)USPS NCOA — licensed data, not free
Best real proxy for in-migrationCSU new utility accounts — CORA pending
Best real proxy for out-migrationCSU closed utility accounts — CORA pending
Military population at Fort CarsonFOIA — Dept of Army
Voter registration new + cancelled by yearCO Secretary of State — sos.state.co.us
The DOLA county births-deaths-migration lookup tool is free, no login required, and returns real vital statistics data combined with Census migration estimates clearly labeled by source. Start there before filing any records requests for this data.

Get email updates

We'll email you a plain-language summary with links to the source documents whenever this office posts new public-record material.

By subscribing you agree to receive emails about this topic. We'll send a confirmation link first. Unsubscribe any time. (consent v2026-04-30-v1)
